title: Wet-felted oven glove
subtitle: A thick, fulled wool oven glove that protects hands to at least 200 degrees Celsius
excerpt: Wet-felt a thick, double-layered oven glove from merino roving. Fully fulled wool insulates to at least 200 degrees Celsius for short oven-door contact. The finished glove fits a standard adult hand and requires about two hours of working time.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. Felted-wool heat protection is historically documented in Mongolian and Central Asian textile traditions; the oven-glove form is a modern British craft adaptation of traditional thick-felt construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together. The more you full the piece, the firmer and denser it becomes.
Vigorous agitation of already-felted wool to shrink and densify it further. A fully fulled piece has very little stretch and is much thicker than the original roving layers.
A waterproof template inside a wet-felted piece that shapes an opening. For the glove, a hand-shaped resist keeps the thumb slot open during fulling.

content.text: compacts the wool fibres into a dense insulating layer. You lay four or five layers of merino roving in a hand shape with a
to preserve the thumb opening, then full until the piece is firm and thick. The key is to full long enough: an under-fulled glove is floppy and insulates poorly.

items.name: Natural or undyed merino roving (100% wool, not superwash)
Hot water (as hot as hands can tolerate)
Thick polythene sheet cut to hand shape (the resist)
Bubble wrap (optional, for additional grip during rubbing)

content.text: Draw around your hand and lower forearm on polythene, adding 30% all round for shrinkage. Cut out. This is the resist that goes inside the glove to keep the thumb opening clear. The polythene must be thick enough to stay flat inside the glove during wetting and rubbing.

Place the resist on the bamboo mat. Lay roving in four layers over the resist: each layer at 90 degrees to the previous (first layer vertical, second horizontal, and so on). Pull the roving into thin wisps rather than working in thick clumps so each layer is even. Leave 3 cm of roving extending past the resist edges; you will fold these over later. Cover the entire hand and forearm section of the resist.

Pour hot soapy water over the roving layers until they are saturated. Press gently to remove air bubbles, then begin rubbing in circular motions with the flat of your hand. Rub for 10 minutes. The roving will start to consolidate into a rough felt surface. Fold the extending edge roving over the resist and onto the back, wetting as you go.
Step 4: Add four more layers on the back

Turn the whole piece over. Lay four more roving layers on the back of the resist in the same cross-hatch pattern. Wet, press, and rub for another 10 minutes. The glove now has wool on both sides of the resist.

Roll the glove in the bamboo mat and roll it back and forth firmly for 5 minutes. Unroll, turn the glove 90 degrees, re-roll and roll for another 5 minutes. Repeat this quarter-turn rolling process four times, then remove the resist by cutting a small opening at the wrist and pulling the polythene out. The glove will loosen slightly once the resist is out. Continue fulling by throwing the glove onto a hard surface (a sink base works well) or hand-rolling it firmly until it is dense, stiff, and has shrunk to fit the hand snugly.

Rinse in cold water, shape the glove onto a hand (or stuff it with a towel), and leave to dry flat away from direct heat. Once dry, the glove is ready to use. Wash by hand in cool water with wool wash; machine washing will shrink and stiffen it further and may cause uneven fulling.

items.problem: The glove is floppy and the roving layers separate in places
items.cause: Under-fulling: the roving was not agitated long enough for the fibres to lock together
items.fix: Continue fulling by hand-rolling or throwing the glove. An oven glove needs extended fulling; 40 to 60 minutes of total working time is normal for a piece this thick.

items.problem: The thumb hole has closed up during fulling
items.cause: The resist moved or compressed during rolling, allowing the wool to close around the thumb gap
items.fix: Insert a finger into the thumb while the piece is still damp and stretch the opening back. Wet wool is very forgiving; re-shape and allow to dry on a former.
